author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

originalBook: In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.
title: Chapter 26

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 26.—60.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "For if you mix what is false with what is true, falsehood often imitates the truth by treading in its steps.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Just in the same way a picture imitates the true man of nature, depicting with its colors the false resemblance of truth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And in the same way, too, the brilliancy of a mirror catches the countenance, so as to represent the eyes of him who gazes on it.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In this way it presents to each comer his own countenance, so that the very features of the comer meet themselves in turn; and of such virtue is the falsehood of a clear mirror, that the very eyes which see themselves recognize themselves as though in some one else.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And even when a shadow stands before it, it doubles the reflection, dividing its unity in great part through a falsehood.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Must we then hold that anything is true, because a lying representation is given of it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But it is one thing to paint a man, another to give birth to one.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For does any one represent fictitious children to a man who wishes for an heir? or would any one look for true heirs in the falsehood of a picture?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Truly it is a proof of madness to fall in love with a picture, letting go one’s hold of what is true."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: Are you then really not ashamed to call the baptism of Christ a lie, even when it is found in the most false of men?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Far be it from any one to suppose that the wheat of the Lord, which has been commanded to grow among the tares throughout the whole field, that is, throughout the whole of this world, until the harvest, that is, until the end of the world, can have perished in consequence of your evil words.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Nay, even among the very tares themselves, which are commanded not to be gathered, but to be tolerated even to the end, and among the very chaff, which shall only be separated from the wheat by the winnowing at the last day, does any one dare to say that any baptism is false which is given and received in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Would you say that those whom you depose from their office, whether as your colleagues or your fellow-priests, on the testimony of women whom they have seduced (since examples of this kind are not wanting anywhere), were false or true before their crime was proved against them?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You will certainly answer, False.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why then were they able both to have and to give true baptism?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why did not their falseness as men corrupt in them the truth of God?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it not most truly written, "For the Holy Spirit of discipline will flee deceit?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Seeing then that the Holy Spirit fled from them, how came it that the truth of baptism was in them, except because what the Holy Spirit fled from was the falseness of man, not the truth of the sacrament?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Further, if even the deceitful have the true baptism, how do they have it who possess it in truthfulness?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence you ought to observe that it is rather your conversation which is colored with childish pigments; and accordingly, he who neglects the living Word to take pleasure in such coloring is himself loving the picture in the place of the reality.
